content:
Ideas of **co-variation** grew alongside early probability and error theory. **Francis Galton** studied "co-relation" in the late 19th century while exploring heredity and regression—he described how two traits vary together and linked correlation to linear prediction.

**Karl Pearson** formalized the **product-moment correlation coefficient** (Pearson's r) around 1896, connecting sample cross-products to standardized association. The same era saw **covariance** enter multivariate statistics as the building block of **covariance matrices** in Gaussian (normal) distribution theory.

In the 20th century, covariance structure became central to **multivariate analysis**, **econometrics**, and **signal processing**. **Mahalanobis** distance (1930s) used inverse covariance to measure statistical distance accounting for correlations.

Modern machine learning treats sample covariance and correlation as everyday objects: EDA heatmaps, PCA on Σ, regularized covariance estimates (shrinkage), and correlation-based feature pruning—all descendants of Pearson–Galton lineage, now computed in one line of NumPy or pandas.

timeline.label: Galton's co-relation
timeline.description: Galton visualized and named co-relation while studying inherited traits and linear prediction between variables.

timeline.label: Pearson's r
timeline.description: Pearson defined the product-moment correlation coefficient, standardizing covariance by marginal spreads.

timeline.label: Multivariate covariance
timeline.description: Covariance matrices underpin multivariate normal models, least squares, and spectral methods like PCA.

timeline.label: ML pipelines
timeline.description: Libraries expose `cov`, `corrcoef`, and `DataFrame.corr` as standard tools for feature analysis and preprocessing.
